summary: German Romantic art movement active in the early 19th century, formally called the Brotherhood of St. Luke (Lukasbund). Founded in Vienna in 1809, then moved to Rome. Sought to revive the religious spirit of medieval and early Renaissance art, especially Raphael. Known for reviving fresco painting. Named for their long hair and biblical clothing. Key members include Johann Friedrich Overbeck and Peter von Cornelius. Goethe criticized them as "retrograde."

works.name: The Nazarenes (Movement Overview)
works.description: German Romantic art movement formally known as the Brotherhood of St. Luke (Lukasbund), founded in Vienna in 1809 and later based in Rome. Members sought to revive the religious nature of early Renaissance painters like Raphael. They revived fresco painting as a medium. Named for their distinctive long hair and biblical clothing. Goethe scoffed at the "retrograde" nature of the group. Wikipedia

works.name: Raphael as Model
works.description: Raphael was the primary artistic model for the Nazarenes. His early works especially inspired their aesthetic ideals of spiritual purity and Renaissance religious painting. The Sistine Madonna, painted by Raphael and housed in Dresden, was notably inspirational to German Romantic artists of this circle. Raphael — Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
The Nazarenes were a German Romantic art movement formally known as the Brotherhood of St. Luke (Lukasbund), founded in Vienna in 1809 by a group of young German and Austrian artists dissatisfied with the rationalist academicism of their era. They subsequently relocated to Rome, where they lived and worked communally in an abandoned monastery. The movement took its popular name from the long hair and biblical dress its members adopted in emulation of early Christian and medieval figures.

The Nazarenes sought to revive the sincere religious spirit they saw in the art of the Middle Ages and the early Italian Renaissance — particularly in the work of Raphael, whose early paintings they viewed as models of pure spiritual expression. A central artistic ambition was the revival of fresco painting, a medium they associated with the communal and devotional art of earlier centuries. Key members included Johann Friedrich Overbeck, a founding figure, and Peter von Cornelius, both of whom produced large-scale religious frescoes in keeping with the group's ideals.

The movement attracted criticism from some of their most illustrious contemporaries. Goethe famously scoffed at the "retrograde" nature of the Nazarenes, seeing their medievalism as a retreat from the progressive values of the Enlightenment. Despite such criticisms, the Nazarenes were influential in 19th-century German art and had a notable impact on the Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood in England, which similarly sought to return to pre-academic religious sincerity in art.
